hero.headline: State income tax and unemployment insurance in Nevada
hero.subhead: No state income tax — MBT and UI are the real numbers.

cost_block.question: Does Nevada have a state income tax for employers?
cost_block.answer_first: No. Nevada's constitution prohibits income tax on natural persons, and there's no corporate income tax either. Your payroll cost stack has two Nevada-specific layers: the Modified Business Tax and Unemployment Insurance.
cost_block.body: Nevada's constitutional prohibition on income tax means you withhold nothing at the state level. No state income tax tables, no state W-2 reconciliation filing, no state withholding account to register. That's the simplest employer payroll administration stack in the US. The real employer costs are the Modified Business Tax (1.17% quarterly on wages above $50,000, less health benefit deductions), Unemployment Insurance (3.00% for new employers on the first $43,700 per employee per year), and mandatory workers' compensation insurance averaging around $0.84 per $100 of payroll. Add federal FICA at 7.65% and FUTA at 0.6% on the first $7,000, and a typical Nevada employee costs roughly 9.5% to 12% above gross salary in employer-side taxes — compared to 15%+ in states with income tax withholding obligations on top. Teamed's fee is $599 per employee per month, flat, with Zero FX mark-up in any currency pairing. Statutory costs pass through at cost, itemised on every invoice.

mbt_block.question: What is Nevada's Modified Business Tax and how is it calculated?
mbt_block.answer_first: The MBT is a 1.17% quarterly payroll tax on gross wages above $50,000, after deducting employer-paid health insurance costs. Financial institutions pay 1.554% with no exemption.
mbt_block.body: Every Nevada employer registered under the Unemployment Compensation Law (NRS 612) must file an MBT return quarterly, even if no tax is owed. The $50,000 quarterly wage exemption means small-payroll employers often owe nothing — a business paying $200,000 a year in wages has only $50,000 of taxable wages per quarter ($200K / 4 = $50K), and the first $50K is exempt, so the MBT bill is zero. At $400,000 annual wages ($100,000 per quarter), the calculation is ($100,000 minus health deductions minus $50,000) x 1.17%. Health benefit deductions include employer-paid premiums for health, dental, and vision. One change to watch in 2026: the Nevada Department of Taxation resumed its MBT Wage Comparison program on 1st January 2026, cross-referencing wages on MBT returns against wages reported to DETR for UI. If your MBT wages and UI wages don't reconcile, expect an audit letter. Teamed handles MBT and UI filings as a unified quarterly process, so your figures always match.

ui_block.question: What are Nevada's unemployment insurance tax rates in 2026?
ui_block.answer_first: New Nevada employers pay 3.00% (2.95% base + 0.05% CEP surcharge) on the first $43,700 per employee per year. Maximum new-employer UI cost is $1,311 per employee annually.
ui_block.body: The $43,700 taxable wage base for 2026 is up from $41,800 in 2025 — it adjusts annually at two-thirds of Nevada's average annual wage (NRS 612). New employers keep the 3.00% rate for 14 to 17 calendar quarters, after which the Nevada Employment Security Division calculates an experience rate. Experience rates range from 0.30% to 5.40%. The 0.05% Career Enhancement Program (CEP) surcharge applies to all employers on top of the base rate and funds workforce retraining. Quarterly returns are filed through Nevada's NUI employer portal. The same 1st January 2026 MBT Wage Comparison update means UI wages and MBT wages will be cross-checked quarterly — mismatches generate follow-up from both DETR and the Department of Taxation. Your Teamed specialist files both returns from the same payroll data set, so the two systems always agree.

wage_hour_block.question: What are Nevada's minimum wage and overtime rules in 2026?
wage_hour_block.answer_first: Nevada's minimum wage is $12.00 per hour for all employers, unified since July 2024. Daily overtime after 8 hours applies only to employees earning under $18.00 per hour.
wage_hour_block.body: The two-tier minimum wage system Nevada operated from 2006 through 30th June 2024 — where employers offering qualifying health insurance could pay $1/hr less — is gone. Every Nevada employee is entitled to $12.00/hr regardless of benefits offered. Legacy compliance guides still reference the old tiered rates; if you've relied on out-of-date materials, review your offer letters. Nevada's overtime rules (NRS 608.018) layer on top of federal FLSA. Weekly overtime applies after 40 hours at 1.5x for all employees. Daily overtime — 1.5x after 8 hours in a single workday — applies only to employees earning less than 1.5 times the state minimum wage (below $18.00/hr as of 2026). Nevada defines a workday as any 24 consecutive hours, not a calendar day (NRS 608.0126). Employers must provide a 10-minute rest break for each 4 hours worked and a 30-minute meal break after 8 continuous hours. A real Teamed operations specialist, not a portal and a chatbot, handles your Nevada compliance questions on these nuances.

benefits_block.question: What leave and benefits must Nevada employers provide?
benefits_block.answer_first: Nevada mandates paid leave (any reason) for employers with 50 or more employees at 40 hours per year. There's no state PFML and no state disability insurance.
benefits_block.body: Nevada SB 312 (NRS 608.0197) requires private employers with 50 or more Nevada employees who have been in business more than two years to provide up to 40 hours of paid leave per year, usable for any reason — illness, personal errands, or anything else. The accrual rate is 0.01923 hours per hour worked, reaching the 40-hour cap for full-time employees by mid-year. Employees can carry over up to 40 hours; employers who frontload the full 40 hours at year start eliminate carryover. Employees may use up to half of their accrued leave for immediate family members. Nevada has no state-funded paid family and medical leave program and no state short-term disability insurance. Employers of 50 or more employees trigger federal FMLA (12 weeks unpaid protected leave), which runs concurrently with SB 312 leave. The 2026 AB 305 update caps FMLA certification fees from healthcare providers at $30 per form. There are no mandatory public holidays for private employers — you set your own holiday policy.

termination_block.question: How do you terminate an employee in Nevada?
termination_block.answer_first: Nevada is at-will. You can end employment for any legal reason. On termination, you must pay all wages immediately — same day as discharge (NRS 608.020).
termination_block.body: Nevada's at-will rule means either party can end employment for any reason, with no required notice period for most private-sector employees. Employment contracts may create notice obligations; check the offer letter. The most operationally important Nevada termination rule is the final paycheck timing. When you discharge an employee, all earned wages become due and payable immediately — on the day of termination, not at the next pay cycle. Voluntary resignations have more flexibility: pay by the next regularly scheduled payday or within 7 days of the resignation date, whichever is earlier. Miss those deadlines and NRS 608.040 allows the employee to claim up to 30 days' additional wages as a penalty. Nevada has no statutory severance requirement; any severance is contractual. Teamed manages payroll offboarding including same-day final pay calculations, benefits discontinuation, and record retention for Nevada employment law compliance.
termination_block.final_pay_quit: Next payday or 7 days, whichever is earlier

worker_classification_block.question: How does Nevada classify independent contractors?
worker_classification_block.answer_first: Nevada uses a '123 test' under NRS 608.0155 for wage and hour purposes. Misclassification penalties run $2,500 per employee (first offence) and $5,000 per employee thereafter.
worker_classification_block.body: NRS 608.0155 creates a three-part test for independent contractor status under Nevada's wage and hour laws. The relationship must satisfy all three: the person is free from direction and control over their work (by contract and in fact); the service falls outside the hirer's usual course of business or is performed away from the hirer's premises; and the person operates in an established independent trade or business. One important limitation: for minimum wage claims arising under Nevada's Constitutional Minimum Wage Amendment, courts apply the federal economic realities test instead — which is harder for employers to satisfy. The Labor Commissioner enforces misclassification penalties at $2,500 for the first misclassified employee and $5,000 for each subsequent one. Before you engage Nevada contractors, run Teamed's Contractor Classifier to check the relationship against the NRS 608.0155 criteria and the economic realities factors simultaneously. One platform, one workflow, contractor through EOR to entity.

good_to_know.question: What do out-of-state companies most often get wrong about Nevada payroll?
good_to_know.body: Three things catch out-of-state employers. First, the MBT Wage Comparison program resumed on 1st January 2026 — your quarterly MBT return and your UI wage report now get cross-checked by two different state agencies. File them from the same data source or expect audit correspondence. Second, the two-tier minimum wage ended in 2024 but outdated HR materials still circulate the old $11/$12 split; any Nevada offer letter that references health-insurance-contingent pay rates is non-compliant. Third, SB 317 changes the workers' compensation payroll cap from $36,000 to approximately $98,400 per employee effective 1st October 2026 — employers with higher-salary Nevada hires will see premium increases at the next renewal cycle.

faq_schema.q: Does Nevada have a state income tax for employees in 2026?
faq_schema.a: No. Nevada's constitution prohibits income tax on natural persons. Employers do not withhold state income tax in Nevada. The main employer payroll taxes are the Modified Business Tax (1.17% on quarterly wages above $50,000) and Unemployment Insurance (3.00% new-employer rate on the first $43,700 per employee per year).

faq_schema.q: What is Nevada's Modified Business Tax rate in 2026?
faq_schema.a: General businesses pay 1.17% on quarterly gross wages exceeding $50,000, after deducting employer-paid health benefit costs. Financial institutions pay 1.554% on all wages with no quarterly exemption. Returns are filed quarterly with the Nevada Department of Taxation.

faq_schema.q: What is Nevada's unemployment insurance taxable wage base for 2026?
faq_schema.a: The Nevada UI taxable wage base for 2026 is $43,700 per employee per year, up from $41,800 in 2025. New employers pay a combined rate of 3.00% (2.95% base rate plus 0.05% Career Enhancement Program surcharge) for approximately 14 to 17 quarters before experience rating applies.

faq_schema.q: What is the Nevada minimum wage in 2026?
faq_schema.a: Nevada's minimum wage is $12.00 per hour for all employees as of 2026. The two-tier system that allowed employers offering qualifying health insurance to pay $1.00 less per hour was eliminated on July 1, 2024. All Nevada employees are entitled to the unified $12.00 rate regardless of benefits offered.

meta.title: Nevada state income tax and unemployment insurance 2026
meta.description: Nevada has no state income tax. Employers pay MBT at 1.17% on wages above $50K/quarter and UI at 3.00% on the first $43,700. Full 2026 employer guide.
